design_philosophy.core_loop: Turn-based civilization building: explore world, found cities, research technologies, manage resources, engage in diplomacy/warfare, and compete against AI civilizations across millennia
design_philosophy.primary_tension: Long-term strategic competition with AI opponents pursuing different victory conditions; resource scarcity and technological arms race create pressure for optimal decision-making
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: Lead a civilization from ancient times to the future, making sweeping decisions about government, expansion, research priorities, and military strategy while competing against historical civilizations
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: Establishes the 4X genre template (eXplore, eXpand, eXploit, eXterminate) with emphasis on wide-ranging player agency in long-form strategic thinking; micromanagement required but not overwhelming; technology tree as primary driver of game progression and competitive advantage; historical civilizations with distinct AI personalities create asymmetric competitive dynamics

difficulty.checkpointing: Turn-based saves available; player can reload at will
difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: Difficulty setting affects end-date (easier settings extend to AD 2100), implying tunable game length and complexity; choice of civilization affects starting position and available resources; player controls research pace, tax rates, government forms, and military strategy

progression.upgrade_system: Technology tree: players research advances from pottery/wheel/alphabet toward nuclear fission and spaceflight; each technology unlocks new units, city improvements, and derivative technologies; first-mover advantage on key techs creates strategic opportunities
progression.unlock_mechanism: Technologies unlock sequentially via research priority; terrain improvements (mines, irrigation, roads, railroads) unlock with tech progression and provide gameplay expansion; new unit types and city improvements tied to tech advancement
replayability_hooks: Multiple selectable civilizations with distinct AI personalities and starting positions
Customizable difficulty settings and game length
Random world generation mode vs. historical 'Play on Earth' map
Multiple victory conditions (implicit in competition with AI civilizations)
Asymmetric starting resources based on civilization and map choice

world_building_techniques: Historical civilization selection (Americans, Aztecs, Babylonians, Chinese, Egyptians, English, French, Germans, Greeks, Indians, Mongols, Romans, Russians, Zulus) with named historical leaders (e.g., Gandhi for India)
Technology progression spanning 4000 BC to AD 2100+ mirrors real historical development
Civilization-specific traits reflected in AI behavior (e.g., Aztecs as fiercely expansionist and wealthy)
Musical heralds and titles change per civilization selection
Barbarian encounters from unclaimed lands provide implicit world history
Terrain and resource availability ground play in geographic/historical logic

faction_indicators: Player-selected civilization with historical identity and AI personality
Computer-controlled civilizations with named leaders and civilization-specific trait expressions
Diplomatic relationships: alliances vs. war declarations
Government form choices affect civilization identity
danger_signals: Barbarian unit harassment from unclaimed territories (decreases as world is explored/settled)
Rival civilizations pursuing aggressive expansion or military advancement
Technological disadvantage relative to competing civilizations
environmental_storytelling: Terrain types and resource distribution define strategic geography
City placement and settler movement reveal player's expansion strategy
Visible terrain improvements (roads, railroads, irrigation, mines) show civilization development
Military unit positions indicate threat levels and territorial control
